narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 23 (well under the usual 36.7 average), Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at 5 (around or slightly below averages of 5.71 and 5.53), and your plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 54% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. Among all the factors considered, your Year 3 school decile ranking had the strongest positive influence, actually pushing the prediction toward bar passage rather than failure, suggesting your relative performance in later undergraduate years stood out as a relative strength compared to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which significantly pulled the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, as your lower-than-average result raised concerns about readiness for the rigors ahead. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, also nudging the outlook toward failure, reinforcing worries about consistent academic strength when benchmarked against typical admitted students. While your full-time enrollment plan aligns with the vast majority (over 92%) of students in the dataset and didn't sway things much, these top three elements—your later-year decile helping you, but LSAT and GPA working against—dominated the model's close-call decision. Compared to typical students who get approved, your test scores and GPA are notably lower, which often signals higher bar failure risk in the data patterns the model has seen. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on historical outcomes for similar profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your Year 3 improvements. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds favorably, and exploring part-time options or other schools with holistic reviews might open doors too. You're clearly motivated—use this feedback to build a stronger case next time.
